title: Use ratios in right triangles
description: Relate angles and side lengths using side length ratios in similar right triangles.

assessment_items.question_content:
Given the measure of an acute angle in a right triangle, we can tell the ratios of the lengths of the triangle's sides relative to that acute angle.

Here are the approximate ratios for angle measures $55\degree$, $65\degree$, and $75\degree$.

Angle | $55\degree$ | $65\degree$ | $75\degree$
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$\dfrac{\text{adjacent leg length}}{\text{hypotenuse length}}$ | $0.57$ | $0.42$ | $0.26$
$\dfrac{\text{opposite leg length}}{\text{hypotenuse length}}$ | $0.82$ | $0.91$ | $0.97$
$\dfrac{\text{opposite leg length}}{\text{adjacent leg length}}$ | $1.43$ | $2.14$ | $3.73$

**Use the table to approximate $AB$ in the triangle below.**



[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The side whose length we want to find, $\maroonD{\overline{AB}}$, is the hypotenuse since it is across from the right angle. 

The side whose length we know, $\blueE{\overline{BC}}$, is the side opposite to the given angle, $\angle A$.



[[☃ image 1]]

We can use the ratio of these side lengths to calculate the length $\maroonD{AB}$.

The given angle measures $55\degree$, and we need to relate the opposite side to the hypotenuse.

Angle | $\purpleD{55\degree}$ | $65\degree$ | $75\degree$
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$\dfrac{\text{adjacent leg length}}{\text{hypotenuse length}}$ | $0.57$ | $0.42$ | $0.26$
$\purpleD{\dfrac{\text{opposite leg length}}{\text{hypotenuse length}}}$ | $\boxed{\purpleD{0.82}}$ | $0.91$ | $0.97$
$\dfrac{\text{opposite leg length}}{\text{adjacent leg length}}$ | $1.43$ | $2.14$ | $3.73$

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{\dfrac{\text{opposite leg length}}{\text{hypotenuse length}}} &\approx \purpleD{0.82}\\\\
\dfrac{BC}{AB} &\approx \purpleD{0.82}\\\\
\dfrac{5}{AB} &\approx \purpleD{0.82}\\\\
\cancel{AB}\cdot \dfrac{5}{\cancel{AB}} &\approx AB\cdot \purpleD{0.82}\\\\
\dfrac{5}{\purpleD{0.82}} &\approx \dfrac{AB\cdot \cancel{\purpleD{0.82}}}{\cancel{\purpleD{0.82}}}\\\\
6.1 &\approx AB
\end{align}$
The best estimate for $AB$ is $6.1$ units.

options.alt: Triangle A B C. Side B C is five units. Angle C is a right angle. Angle A is fifty-five degrees.

assessment_items.question_content:
Given the measure of an acute angle in a right triangle, we can tell the ratios of the lengths of the triangle's sides relative to that acute angle.

Here are the approximate ratios for angle measures $55\degree$, $65\degree$, and $75\degree$.

Angle | $55\degree$ | $65\degree$ | $75\degree$
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$\dfrac{\text{adjacent leg length}}{\text{hypotenuse length}}$ | $0.57$ | $0.42$ | $0.26$
$\dfrac{\text{opposite leg length}}{\text{hypotenuse length}}$ | $0.82$ | $0.91$ | $0.97$
$\dfrac{\text{opposite leg length}}{\text{adjacent leg length}}$ | $1.43$ | $2.14$ | $3.73$

**Use the table to approximate $GH$ in the triangle below.**



[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The side whose length we want to find, $\blueE{\overline{GH}}$, is the side opposite to the given angle, $\angle I$. 

The side whose length we know, $\goldE{\overline{HI}}$, is the side adjacent to the given angle.



[[☃ image 1]]

We can use the ratio of these side lengths to calculate the length $\blueE{GH}$.

The given angle measures $65\degree$, and we need to relate the opposite side to the adjacent side.

Angle | $55\degree$ | $\purpleD{65\degree}$ | $75\degree$
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$\dfrac{\text{adjacent leg length}}{\text{hypotenuse length}}$ | $0.57$ | $0.42$ | $0.26$
$\dfrac{\text{opposite leg length}}{\text{hypotenuse length}}$ | $0.82$ | $0.91$ | $0.97$
$\purpleD{\dfrac{\text{opposite leg length}}{\text{adjacent leg length}}}$ | $1.43$ | $\boxed{\purpleD{2.14}}$ | $3.73$

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{\dfrac{\text{opposite leg length}}{\text{adjacent leg length}}} &\approx \purpleD{2.14}\\\\
\dfrac{GH}{HI} &\approx \purpleD{2.14}\\\\
\dfrac{GH}{3} &\approx \purpleD{2.14}\\\\
\cancel{3}\cdot \dfrac{GH}{\cancel{3}} &\approx 3\cdot \purpleD{2.14}\\\\
GH &\approx 6.4
\end{align}$
The best estimate for $GH$ is $6.4$ units.

options.alt: Triangle G H I. Side H I is three units. Angle H is a right angle. Angle I is sixty-five degrees.

assessment_items.question_content:
Given the measure of an acute angle in a right triangle, we can tell the ratios of the lengths of the triangle's sides relative to that acute angle.

Here are the approximate ratios for angle measures $55\degree$, $65\degree$, and $75\degree$.

Angle | $55\degree$ | $65\degree$ | $75\degree$
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$\dfrac{\text{adjacent leg length}}{\text{hypotenuse length}}$ | $0.57$ | $0.42$ | $0.26$
$\dfrac{\text{opposite leg length}}{\text{hypotenuse length}}$ | $0.82$ | $0.91$ | $0.97$
$\dfrac{\text{opposite leg length}}{\text{adjacent leg length}}$ | $1.43$ | $2.14$ | $3.73$

**Use the table to approximate $MO$ in the triangle below.**



[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The side whose length we want to find, $\maroonD{\overline{MO}}$, is the hypotenuse since it is across from the right angle. 

The side whose length we know, $\goldE{\overline{NO}}$, is the side adjacent to the given angle, $\angle O$.



[[☃ image 1]]

We can use the ratio of these side lengths to calculate the length $\maroonD{MO}$.

The given angle measures $75\degree$, and we need to relate the adjacent side to the hypotenuse.

Angle | $55\degree$ | $65\degree$ | $\purpleD{75\degree}$
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$\purpleD{\dfrac{\text{adjacent leg length}}{\text{hypotenuse length}}}$ | $0.57$ | $0.42$ | $\boxed{\purpleD{0.26}}$
$\dfrac{\text{opposite leg length}}{\text{hypotenuse length}}$ | $0.82$ | $0.91$ | $0.97$
$\dfrac{\text{opposite leg length}}{\text{adjacent leg length}}$ | $1.43$ | $2.14$ | $3.73$

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{\dfrac{\text{adjacent leg length}}{\text{hypotenuse length}}} &\approx \purpleD{0.26}\\\\
\dfrac{NO}{MO} &\approx \purpleD{0.26}\\\\
\dfrac{2}{MO} &\approx \purpleD{0.26}\\\\
\cancel{MO}\cdot \dfrac{2}{\cancel{MO}} &\approx MO\cdot \purpleD{0.26}\\\\
\dfrac{2}{\purpleD{0.26}} &\approx \dfrac{MO\cdot \cancel{\purpleD{0.26}}}{\cancel{\purpleD{0.26}}}\\\\
7.7 &\approx MO
\end{align}$
The best estimate for $MO$ is $7.7$ units.

options.alt: Triangle M N O. Side N O is two units. Angle N is a right angle. Angle O is seventy-five degrees.

assessment_items.question_content:
Given the measure of an acute angle in a right triangle, we can tell the ratios of the lengths of the triangle's sides relative to that acute angle.

Here are the approximate ratios for angle measures $55\degree$, $65\degree$, and $75\degree$.

Angle | $55\degree$ | $65\degree$ | $75\degree$
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$\dfrac{\text{adjacent leg length}}{\text{hypotenuse length}}$ | $0.57$ | $0.42$ | $0.26$
$\dfrac{\text{opposite leg length}}{\text{hypotenuse length}}$ | $0.82$ | $0.91$ | $0.97$
$\dfrac{\text{opposite leg length}}{\text{adjacent leg length}}$ | $1.43$ | $2.14$ | $3.73$

**Use the table to approximate $m\angle D$ in the triangle below.**



[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We are given the lengths of two sides, $\blueE{\overline{EF}}$ and $\maroonD{\overline{DE}}$:

- $\blueE{\overline{EF}}$ is the side opposite to the angle whose measure we want to find, $\angle D$.
- $\maroonD{\overline{DE}}$ is the hypotenuse since it is across from the right angle.



[[☃ image 1]]

We can use the ratio of these side lengths to approximate $m\angle D$.

We need to relate the opposite side to the hypotenuse.

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{\text{opposite leg length}}{\text{hypotenuse length}} &= \dfrac{EF}{DE}\\\\
&\approx \dfrac{6.1}{6.7}\\\\
&\approx 0.91
\end{align}$

Let's find the ratio nearest to $0.91$ in the $\purpleD{\dfrac{\text{opposite leg length}}{\text{hypotenuse length}}}$ row.

Angle | $55\degree$ | $\purpleD{65\degree}$ | $75\degree$
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$\dfrac{\text{opposite leg length}}{\text{hypotenuse length}}$ | $0.82$ | $\boxed{\purpleD{0.91}}$ | $0.97$

The nearest ratio value is in the $\purpleD{65\degree}$ column. So $65\degree$ is a pretty good estimate for $m\angle D$.
The best estimate for $m\angle D$ is $65\degree$.

options.alt: Triangle D E F. Side E F is six point one units. Angle F is a right angle. Angle D is the angle of reference. Side D E is six point seven units.

$\dfrac{\text{opposite leg length}}{\text{hypotenuse length}}$ | $0.82$ | $0.91$ | $0.97$

**Use the table to approximate $m\angle L$ in the triangle below.**



[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We are given the lengths of two sides, $\blueE{\overline{JK}}$ and $\goldE{\overline{KL}}$:

- $\blueE{\overline{JK}}$ is the side opposite to the angle whose measure we want to find, $\angle L$.
- $\goldE{\overline{KL}}$ is the side adjacent to $\angle L$.



[[☃ image 1]]

We can use the ratio of these side lengths to approximate $m\angle L$.

We need to relate the opposite side to the adjacent side.

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{\text{opposite leg length}}{\text{adjacent leg length}} &= \dfrac{JK}{KL}\\\\
&\approx \dfrac{11.9}{3.2}\\\\
&\approx 3.7
\end{align}$

Let's find the ratio nearest to $3.7$ in the $\purpleD{\dfrac{\text{opposite leg length}}{\text{adjacent leg length}}}$ row.

Angle | $55\degree$ | $65\degree$ | $\purpleD{75\degree}$
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$\dfrac{\text{opposite leg length}}{\text{adjacent leg length}}$ | $1.43$ | $2.14$ | $\boxed{\purpleD{3.73}}$

The nearest ratio value is in the $\purpleD{75\degree}$ column. So $75\degree$ is a pretty good estimate for $m\angle L$.
The best estimate for $m\angle L$ is $75\degree$.

options.alt: Triangle J K L. Side J K is eleven point nine units. Angle K is a right angle. Angle L is the angle of reference. Side K L is three point two units.

assessment_items.question_content:
Right triangles $\boxed{1}$, $\boxed{2}$, and $\boxed{3}$ are given with all their angle measures and approximate side lengths.



[[☃ image 1]]

**Use one of the triangles to approximate the ratio $\dfrac{WY}{WX}$.**



[[☃ image 2]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The key understanding here is that $\triangle WXY$ is similar to triangle $\boxed{1}$ based on the AA criterion (they both have a right angle and a $70\degree$ angle).

We can find $\dfrac{WY}{WX}$ by setting up a proportion statement that includes $WX$, $WY$,  and the lengths of their corresponding sides in triangle $\boxed{1}$.

options.alt: Three triangles labeled one, two, and three. The triangle labeled one has its short leg labeled three point four units. Its long leg is nine point four units. Its hypotenuse is ten units. The angle opposite of the hypotenuse is ninety degrees. The angle opposite of the short leg is twenty degrees. The angle opposite of the long leg is seventy degrees. The triangle labeled two has its short leg labeled five point seven units. Its long leg is eight point two units. Its hypotenuse is ten units. The angle opposite of the hypotenuse is ninety degrees. The angle opposite of the short leg is thirty-five degrees. The angle opposite of the long leg is fifty-five degrees. The triangle labeled three has its short leg labeled six point four units. Its long leg is seven point seven units. Its hypotenuse is ten units. The angle opposite of the hypotenuse is ninety degrees. The angle opposite of the short leg is forty degrees. The angle opposite of the long leg is fifty degrees.
A right triangle W X Y. Angle Y is a right angle. Angle W is seventy degrees.

assessment_items.question_content:
Right triangles $\boxed{1}$, $\boxed{2}$, and $\boxed{3}$ are given with all their angle measures and approximate side lengths.



[[☃ image 1]]

**Use one of the triangles to approximate $EF$ in the triangle below.**



[[☃ image 2]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The key understanding here is that $\triangle DEF$ is similar to triangle $\boxed{1}$ based on the AA criterion (they both have a right angle and a $20\degree$ angle).

We can find $EF$ by setting up a proportion statement that includes $EF$ and other known lengths.

Let's plug $DF=6$ into the equation and solve.

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{EF}{3.4}&=\dfrac{6}{9.4}
\\\\
EF&=\dfrac{6}{9.4}\cdot 3.4
\\\\
EF&\approx 2.2
\end{align}$
The best estimate for $EF$ is $2.2$ units.

The triangle labeled two has its short leg labeled five point seven units. Its long leg is eight point two units. Its hypotenuse is ten units. The angle opposite of the hypotenuse is ninety degrees. The angle opposite of the short leg is thirty-five degrees. The angle opposite of the long leg is fifty-five degrees. The triangle labeled three has its short leg labeled six point four units. Its long leg is seven point seven units. Its hypotenuse is ten units. The angle opposite of the hypotenuse is ninety degrees. The angle opposite of the short leg is forty degrees. The angle opposite of the long leg is fifty degrees.
Triangle D E F has a height of six units, which is side DF. Both the base, side EF, and the hypotenuse, side DE, are unknown. Angle D F E is a right angle and angle F D E measures twenty degrees.

assessment_items.question_content:
Right triangles $\boxed{1}$, $\boxed{2}$, and $\boxed{3}$ are given with all their angle measures and approximate side lengths.

**Use one of the triangles to approximate $MO$ in the triangle below.**


[[☃ image 2]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The key understanding here is that $\triangle MNO$ is similar to triangle $\boxed{2}$ based on the AA criterion (they both have a right angle and a $35\degree$ angle).

We can find $MO$ by setting up a proportion statement that includes $MO$ and other known lengths.

Let's plug $MN=8$ into the equation and solve.

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{MO}{8.2}&=\dfrac{8}{10}
\\\\
MO&=\dfrac{8}{10}\cdot 8.2
\\\\
MO&\approx 6.6
\end{align}$
The best estimate for $MO$ is $6.6$ units.
